title: The problem with agents is usually the difference between an assistant and a dependency
summary: A personal essay about agents, and the longer argument hidden inside the difference between an assistant and a dependency.
topic: agents once maintenance became the real product
body: There was a version of agents circulating in November 2024 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. The trend tends to look obvious only after somebody curates the scene around it. Standing inside a desktop crowded with small useful automations, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

It remains interesting partly because it resists being filed into one neat ideological drawer. Agents can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The mature version of this argument has to make room for both sides of that feeling. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. To me it means asking what habits of obedience and attention the tool installs while we celebrate the convenience.

In 2024, I could not separate the technical story from the social one. These systems were practical, yes, but they were also vehicles for aspiration, identity, and fatigue. People wanted different things from them: less drudgery, more status, clearer judgment, fewer boring tasks. The problem was that social desire kept moving faster than the underlying evidence.

Experience keeps pushing me toward the same habit: notice what disappears in the polished retelling. The missing pieces are rarely mysterious, they are usually labor, repair, emotional strain, governance, failure handling, and uneven burden. Agents looked different to me once I framed it that way. The solution stopped looking inevitable and started looking curated. That does not make the thing useless. It mostly makes the thing legible again, which is often the first real service criticism provides.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me track not only the movement of the technologies but the movement of my own noticing. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting to me is the slower drift in what starts to feel normal, what starts to feel extractive, and what still seems worth wanting. Agents sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from November 2024: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. What matters to me in the future tense is what the system extracts from the people closest to the work. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous framing is usually what keeps the important part out of view.

references.title: The problem with memory systems is usually the difference between an assistant and a dependency
I keep coming back to contactless interfaces
